title: Lesson 416
transcript: We just went through an overview of the risk management process, which is three major steps, right? Asset valuation, risk analysis, and treatment. And here is where we're gonna talk about asset valuation in just a little bit more detail. The reason this is gonna be such a short video is because asset valuation is a very simple process, conceptually. The whole point of asset valuation, right, is that you wanna come up with a big list of all the assets from across the organization And then you want to rank those assets from which are of greatest value on down to least. And this is really helpful for overall risk management because you want to focus most of your time on the most valuable assets. You're not going to spend two days evaluating the risks associated with a paperclip, right? It'd be kind of pointless. But if there's some really high value system, you want to invest a lot of effort into understanding the risks associated with that system. So when you have a big list of assets from all across your organization, you need to know which assets are of greater or lesser value. And that's the whole point of the asset valuation process, trying to rank the assets so you can rank them from greatest on down to least value. There are two major ways that we can perform this asset valuation, qualitative and quantitative analysis. Now, without question, the absolute most desirable way that we would want to do asset valuation is quantitative analysis, where we assign specifically, where we're assigning a monetary value to each asset. That would, without question, be the best way of doing this, where we could say, okay, our most valuable asset is worth $27 billion, and the next one's $22 billion, and this one's $138,000, and this one's $62,558, et cetera. That would be the best way of ranking all the assets by being able to assign an accurate monetary value to them. However, you're not going to be able to do that the vast majority of the time. Because in any organization, a huge number of the assets are nigh impossible to assign a reasonably accurate value to. Like, what's a company's reputation worth? For certain companies, their reputation is worth an immense amount. But can you put an exact dollar value on it? No. Or you look at some system and you're like, wow, that is a critical system, right? The data in that system is worth a fortune. Can you put an exact dollar value on it? No. And so this is true for the majority of assets in the organization. You are not going to be able to assign a reasonably accurate monetary value to them. So despite the fact that we like to use quantitative analysis everywhere and assign a monetary value to every asset, we just can't. There's some assets that you can do it for, but not that many. And that's why we're forced to use qualitative analysis the vast majority of the time. And fundamentally, first line here says, right, qualitative analysis is a relative ranking system. It's where you come up with some criteria. You know, you can still use numbers here. One, two, three, four, five. Or maybe you use names like this is valuable, super valuable, really super valuable, whatever. You come up with some categorization system and then you put assets into those categories and you basically are just ranking assets relative to each other. You're looking at two assets and you're saying, oh, is this one more valuable than that one? and you rank them accordingly. That's what qualitative analysis is. And this is, you know, you can do this without having the exact monetary value. You can use other criteria, like what if this system was unavailable? What if this data was exposed? What would the impact be to the business if this system was unavailable, et cetera? You can ask these sorts of questions, compare systems, and therefore rank your assets qualitatively as to which of the greatest value on down to the least. So conceptually, a very simple process can take a while. Just one final reminder, we would prefer to do quantitative everywhere. We're forced to use qualitative most of the time. Thank you.
